questions.question: Explain VM Escape attacks. How do they work, what is their impact, and how can they be mitigated?
keyPoints: VM Escape is when an attacker inside a Guest VM exploits a hypervisor vulnerability to break out of VM isolation.
Typically exploits bugs in virtual hardware emulation (virtual NIC, virtual display, virtual floppy controller).
Impact: attacker gains access to the host and potentially ALL VMs on that host — the most severe virtualization attack.
Real-world example: CVE-2015-3456 (VENOM) exploited the virtual floppy disk controller in QEMU/KVM/Xen.
Mitigation: keep hypervisor patched, minimize virtual hardware exposed to VMs, use hardware-assisted isolation (VT-x, VT-d), apply micro-segmentation.

questions.question: Describe the VM isolation mechanisms and explain the security risks of live migration and snapshots.
keyPoints: CPU Isolation: Intel VT-x/AMD-V hardware extensions provide separate execution contexts per VM.
Memory Isolation: hypervisor allocates separate memory pages, preventing cross-VM memory access.
I/O Isolation: Intel VT-d and SR-IOV ensure VMs have isolated device access.
Network Isolation: virtual switches (vSwitch) and VLANs separate VM traffic.
Live migration risk: VM state (memory with secrets) transferred over network — intercept risk if unencrypted.
Snapshot risk: contains full VM state including encryption keys, session tokens — must be encrypted and access-controlled.

questions.question: What is VM Sprawl? Explain its security implications and mitigation strategies.
keyPoints: VM Sprawl is the uncontrolled proliferation of VMs that are never properly decommissioned.
Forgotten VMs remain unpatched and unmonitored, creating security gaps.
May contain stale credentials, sensitive data, or outdated configurations.
Mitigation: automated expiration policies, regular VM audits, tagging and lifecycle management.
